name: Structure and integrative functions of the main organ systems

nodes.label: Nervous system
nodes.summary: The nervous system uses electrically excitable neurons to rapidly sense, integrate, and coordinate body responses.

nodes.label: Organization of the nervous system
nodes.summary: The nervous system divides into central (brain and spinal cord) and peripheral divisions with somatic and autonomic branches.

nodes.label: CNS vs PNS
nodes.summary: The CNS is the brain and spinal cord; the PNS is all neural tissue outside it including cranial and spinal nerves.

nodes.label: Somatic nervous system
nodes.summary: The somatic nervous system carries voluntary motor commands to skeletal muscle and sensory input from skin and joints.

nodes.label: Autonomic nervous system
nodes.summary: The autonomic nervous system involuntarily regulates smooth muscle, cardiac muscle, and glands via sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions.

nodes.label: Sympathetic division
nodes.summary: The sympathetic (thoracolumbar) division mediates fight-or-flight responses, largely via norepinephrine at target organs.

nodes.label: Parasympathetic division
nodes.summary: The parasympathetic (craniosacral) division mediates rest-and-digest responses via acetylcholine, with the vagus nerve as a major output.

nodes.label: Autonomic ganglia and pre/postganglionic neurons
nodes.summary: Autonomic pathways use a two-neuron chain synapsing in a ganglion; sympathetic ganglia lie near the cord, parasympathetic near the target.

nodes.label: Enteric nervous system
nodes.summary: The enteric nervous system is a semi-autonomous network in the gut wall that controls motility and secretion.

nodes.label: Reflex arc
nodes.summary: A reflex arc is sensor → afferent neuron → integration center → efferent neuron → effector, enabling rapid involuntary responses.

nodes.label: Monosynaptic vs polysynaptic reflex
nodes.summary: A monosynaptic reflex (knee-jerk) has one synapse; polysynaptic reflexes (withdrawal) interpose interneurons.

nodes.label: Gray vs white matter
nodes.summary: Gray matter is unmyelinated cell bodies and synapses; white matter is myelinated axon tracts.

nodes.label: Nerves, tracts, nuclei, and ganglia
nodes.summary: A nerve/tract is a bundle of axons; a ganglion (PNS) or nucleus (CNS) is a cluster of cell bodies.

nodes.label: Neurons and glia
nodes.summary: Neurons are the signaling cells of the nervous system, supported by several glial cell types.

nodes.label: Neuron structure
nodes.summary: A neuron has dendrites (input), a soma (integration), an axon (conduction), and axon terminals (output).

nodes.label: Axon hillock and trigger zone
nodes.summary: The axon hillock sums incoming potentials and is where the action potential is initiated when threshold is reached.

nodes.label: Sensory, motor, interneuron
nodes.summary: Sensory (afferent) neurons carry input inward, motor (efferent) neurons carry commands outward, and interneurons connect within the CNS.

nodes.label: Myelin sheath
nodes.summary: Myelin is a lipid-rich insulating wrap that speeds conduction; made by oligodendrocytes in the CNS and Schwann cells in the PNS.

nodes.label: Nodes of Ranvier
nodes.summary: Nodes of Ranvier are gaps in myelin where action potentials regenerate, enabling fast saltatory conduction.

nodes.label: Glial cells overview
nodes.summary: Glia (astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, Schwann cells, microglia, ependymal) support, insulate, nourish, and protect neurons.

nodes.label: Astrocytes
nodes.summary: Astrocytes support neurons, maintain the extracellular environment, and contribute to the blood-brain barrier.

nodes.label: Microglia
nodes.summary: Microglia are the resident immune phagocytes of the CNS.

nodes.label: Ependymal cells
nodes.summary: Ependymal cells line the ventricles and produce cerebrospinal fluid.

nodes.label: Neuronal signaling
nodes.summary: Neurons signal via electrical action potentials along axons and chemical transmission at synapses.

nodes.label: Resting membrane potential
nodes.summary: The resting potential (~-70 mV) arises from K+ leak channels and the Na+/K+ ATPase maintaining ion gradients.

nodes.label: Na+/K+ ATPase
nodes.summary: The Na+/K+ ATPase pumps 3 Na+ out and 2 K+ in per ATP, sustaining the ion gradients underlying excitability.

nodes.label: Equilibrium potential and Nernst
nodes.summary: Each ion's equilibrium potential (Nernst) is the voltage at which its electrical and chemical gradients balance, setting membrane potential.

nodes.label: Graded potentials
nodes.summary: Graded potentials are small, decremental, summable voltage changes that can trigger an action potential if threshold is reached.

nodes.label: Threshold and all-or-none
nodes.summary: An action potential fires all-or-none only when depolarization reaches threshold (~-55 mV).

nodes.label: Depolarization (Na+ influx)
nodes.summary: Voltage-gated Na+ channels open at threshold, driving rapid depolarization toward the Na+ equilibrium potential.

nodes.label: Repolarization (K+ efflux)
nodes.summary: Na+ channels inactivate and voltage-gated K+ channels open, repolarizing the membrane.

nodes.label: Hyperpolarization (undershoot)
nodes.summary: Slow K+ channel closure transiently drives the membrane below resting potential before recovery.

nodes.label: Refractory periods
nodes.summary: The absolute refractory period (Na+ channels inactivated) prevents re-firing; the relative period requires stronger stimulus.

nodes.label: Action potential propagation and directionality
nodes.summary: The action potential regenerates down the axon and travels one way because the trailing membrane is refractory.

nodes.label: Saltatory conduction
nodes.summary: In myelinated axons the action potential jumps node to node, increasing conduction velocity.

nodes.label: Determinants of conduction velocity
nodes.summary: Conduction speed increases with myelination and larger axon diameter (lower internal resistance).

nodes.label: Chemical vs electrical synapses
nodes.summary: Chemical synapses use neurotransmitters across a cleft; electrical synapses pass current directly through gap junctions.

nodes.label: Synaptic transmission steps
nodes.summary: Action potential → Ca2+ influx → vesicle fusion → neurotransmitter release → receptor binding → postsynaptic response.

nodes.label: Ca2+-triggered vesicle release
nodes.summary: Depolarization opens voltage-gated Ca2+ channels at the terminal, and Ca2+ triggers SNARE-mediated vesicle fusion.

nodes.label: Ionotropic vs metabotropic receptors
nodes.summary: Ionotropic receptors are ligand-gated ion channels (fast); metabotropic receptors act through G proteins (slower, modulatory).

nodes.label: Neurotransmitter clearance
nodes.summary: Signals end via enzymatic degradation, reuptake, or diffusion of neurotransmitter from the cleft.

nodes.label: EPSPs and IPSPs / summation
nodes.summary: Excitatory and inhibitory postsynaptic potentials sum temporally and spatially to determine firing.

nodes.label: Frequency coding of stimulus intensity
nodes.summary: Because action potentials are all-or-none, stimulus strength is encoded by firing frequency and number of neurons recruited.

nodes.label: Neurotransmitters
nodes.summary: Distinct chemical neurotransmitters carry signals across synapses with characteristic effects.

nodes.label: Acetylcholine
nodes.summary: Acetylcholine acts at the neuromuscular junction and in the parasympathetic system; degraded by acetylcholinesterase.

nodes.label: Catecholamines (NE, epinephrine, dopamine)
nodes.summary: Catecholamines mediate sympathetic output, reward, and movement and are synthesized from tyrosine.

nodes.label: Serotonin
nodes.summary: Serotonin (from tryptophan) modulates mood, sleep, and appetite.

nodes.label: GABA and glycine
nodes.summary: GABA and glycine are the major inhibitory neurotransmitters of the CNS.

nodes.label: Glutamate
nodes.summary: Glutamate is the major excitatory neurotransmitter of the CNS.

nodes.label: Neuropeptides and other transmitters
nodes.summary: Endorphins, substance P, and nitric oxide act as neuromodulators affecting pain, mood, and vascular tone.

nodes.label: Sensory reception and processing
nodes.summary: Sensory receptors transduce stimuli into neural signals carried to the CNS for perception.

nodes.label: Sensory receptor types
nodes.summary: Mechanoreceptors, chemoreceptors, photoreceptors, thermoreceptors, and nociceptors detect distinct stimulus modalities.

nodes.label: Sensory transduction and receptor potentials
nodes.summary: A stimulus opens channels to produce a graded receptor potential that, if large enough, triggers afferent action potentials.

nodes.label: Sensory adaptation
nodes.summary: Receptor sensitivity declines with constant stimulation; tonic receptors adapt slowly, phasic receptors quickly.

nodes.label: Proprioception (muscle spindles, GTOs)
nodes.summary: Muscle spindles sense stretch and Golgi tendon organs sense tension, providing feedback for posture and reflexes.

nodes.label: Central nervous system structures
nodes.summary: The brain comprises the forebrain, midbrain, and hindbrain, each with specialized regions.

nodes.label: Cerebral cortex and lobes
nodes.summary: The cerebral cortex's frontal, parietal, temporal, and occipital lobes govern higher cognition, sensation, hearing/memory, and vision.

nodes.label: Hypothalamus
nodes.summary: The hypothalamus maintains homeostasis (temperature, hunger, thirst) and links the nervous and endocrine systems.

nodes.label: Thalamus
nodes.summary: The thalamus is the relay station routing most sensory information to the cortex.

nodes.label: Cerebellum
nodes.summary: The cerebellum coordinates movement, balance, and motor learning.

nodes.label: Brainstem (medulla, pons, midbrain)
nodes.summary: The brainstem controls vital autonomic functions including breathing, heart rate, and arousal.

nodes.label: Limbic system
nodes.summary: The limbic system (amygdala, hippocampus) governs emotion and memory formation.

nodes.label: Spinal cord
nodes.summary: The spinal cord conducts ascending sensory and descending motor tracts and mediates reflexes.

nodes.label: Forebrain, midbrain, hindbrain divisions
nodes.summary: The embryonic brain forms forebrain (cerebrum, thalamus, hypothalamus), midbrain, and hindbrain (cerebellum, pons, medulla).

nodes.label: CSF and meninges
nodes.summary: The meninges and cerebrospinal fluid cushion and protect the CNS.

nodes.label: Blood-brain barrier
nodes.summary: Tight junctions between brain capillary endothelial cells selectively restrict passage into the CNS.

nodes.label: Endocrine system
nodes.summary: The endocrine system secretes hormones into the blood for slow, widespread, long-lasting regulation of homeostasis.

nodes.label: Hormone principles
nodes.summary: Hormones are classified by chemistry and act through cell-surface or intracellular receptors with feedback control.

nodes.label: Peptide hormones
nodes.summary: Water-soluble peptide hormones bind surface receptors and act fast through second messengers; cannot cross membranes.

nodes.label: Steroid hormones
nodes.summary: Lipid-soluble steroid hormones cross the membrane, bind intracellular receptors, and alter gene transcription slowly.

nodes.label: Amino-acid-derived hormones
nodes.summary: Tyrosine-derived hormones include catecholamines (water-soluble) and thyroid hormones (lipid-soluble).

nodes.label: Second messenger signaling
nodes.summary: Peptide hormones use cascades like cAMP/PKA to amplify and rapidly transduce signals.

nodes.label: Hormone transport and half-life
nodes.summary: Lipid-soluble hormones travel bound to carrier proteins and act slowly with long half-lives; peptides circulate free and clear fast.

nodes.label: Target specificity and receptor regulation
nodes.summary: Only cells with the matching receptor respond; up/down-regulation of receptors tunes target sensitivity.

nodes.label: Negative feedback
nodes.summary: Most endocrine axes use negative feedback in which the product inhibits its own upstream stimulus.

nodes.label: Positive feedback
nodes.summary: Positive feedback amplifies a response (e.g., oxytocin in labor, LH surge in ovulation).

nodes.label: Tropic vs direct hormones
nodes.summary: Tropic hormones act on other endocrine glands; direct hormones act on non-endocrine targets.

nodes.label: Endocrine, paracrine, autocrine signaling
nodes.summary: Endocrine signals travel via blood, paracrine act on nearby cells, and autocrine act on the secreting cell itself.

nodes.label: Endocrine glands and hormones
nodes.summary: Each endocrine gland secretes characteristic hormones with specific physiological roles.

nodes.label: Hypothalamus (endocrine)
nodes.summary: The hypothalamus secretes releasing/inhibiting hormones controlling the anterior pituitary and makes ADH and oxytocin.

nodes.label: Hypophyseal portal system
nodes.summary: A portal vein system carries hypothalamic releasing hormones directly to the anterior pituitary.

nodes.label: Anterior pituitary
nodes.summary: The anterior pituitary secretes FSH, LH, ACTH, TSH, prolactin, GH, and endorphins under hypothalamic control.

nodes.label: Growth hormone and prolactin
nodes.summary: GH promotes growth and metabolism (via IGF-1) and prolactin stimulates milk production; both are direct anterior-pituitary hormones.

nodes.label: Posterior pituitary
nodes.summary: The posterior pituitary stores and releases hypothalamus-made ADH and oxytocin.

nodes.label: Thyroid (T3/T4, calcitonin)
nodes.summary: The thyroid secretes T3/T4 to raise basal metabolic rate and calcitonin to lower blood calcium.

nodes.label: Parathyroid (PTH)
nodes.summary: Parathyroid hormone raises blood calcium via bone resorption, renal reabsorption, and vitamin D activation.

nodes.label: Adrenal cortex
nodes.summary: The adrenal cortex secretes glucocorticoids (cortisol), mineralocorticoids (aldosterone), and androgens.

nodes.label: Adrenal medulla
nodes.summary: The adrenal medulla secretes epinephrine and norepinephrine for the sympathetic fight-or-flight response.

nodes.label: Endocrine pancreas (insulin, glucagon)
nodes.summary: Pancreatic islets secrete insulin (lowers glucose) from beta cells and glucagon (raises glucose) from alpha cells.

nodes.label: Somatostatin and islet integration
nodes.summary: Delta-cell somatostatin inhibits insulin and glucagon release, coordinating pancreatic islet output.

nodes.label: Gonads (sex hormones)
nodes.summary: Testes secrete testosterone and ovaries secrete estrogen and progesterone to drive reproduction.

nodes.label: Pineal gland (melatonin)
nodes.summary: The pineal gland secretes melatonin to regulate circadian rhythm.

nodes.label: Other endocrine sources
nodes.summary: The kidney secretes erythropoietin and renin, and the heart secretes ANP, integrating organs into the endocrine system.

nodes.label: Thymus (thymosin)
nodes.summary: The thymus secretes thymosin to support T-cell maturation, linking the endocrine and immune systems.

nodes.label: Endocrine axes and integration
nodes.summary: Hierarchical hypothalamic-pituitary axes coordinate multi-gland hormonal responses.

nodes.label: Hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis
nodes.summary: CRH → ACTH → cortisol drives the stress response with cortisol feeding back negatively.

nodes.label: Hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis
nodes.summary: TRH → TSH → T3/T4 controls metabolism under negative feedback.

nodes.label: Hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis
nodes.summary: GnRH → FSH/LH → sex steroids governs reproduction.

nodes.label: Blood glucose regulation
nodes.summary: Insulin and glucagon act antagonistically to keep blood glucose within a narrow homeostatic range.

nodes.label: Calcium homeostasis
nodes.summary: PTH, calcitonin, and active vitamin D coordinate to keep blood calcium constant.

nodes.label: Integrated stress response
nodes.summary: Fast sympathetic/adrenal-medullary catecholamines and slow cortisol jointly mobilize energy under stress.

nodes.label: Circulatory (cardiovascular) system
nodes.summary: The circulatory system pumps blood through vessels to transport gases, nutrients, wastes, hormones, and heat.

nodes.label: Heart structure and function
nodes.summary: The four-chambered heart is a dual pump driving pulmonary and systemic circulation.

nodes.label: Chambers and valves
nodes.summary: Two atria and two ventricles with AV (tricuspid, mitral) and semilunar (pulmonary, aortic) valves enforce one-way flow.

nodes.label: Path of blood through the heart
nodes.summary: Vena cavae → RA → RV → pulmonary artery → lungs → pulmonary veins → LA → LV → aorta → body.

nodes.label: Pulmonary vs systemic circuits
nodes.summary: The pulmonary circuit oxygenates blood at the lungs; the systemic circuit delivers it to the body.

nodes.label: Cardiac muscle and syncytium
nodes.summary: Cardiac myocytes are connected by intercalated discs with gap junctions, forming a functional syncytium.

nodes.label: Coronary circulation
nodes.summary: Coronary arteries supply the heart muscle itself with oxygenated blood, branching from the aorta.

nodes.label: Portal systems and fetal circulation
nodes.summary: Portal systems (hepatic, hypophyseal) link two capillary beds; fetal shunts bypass the nonfunctional lungs and liver.

nodes.label: Cardiac conduction and cycle
nodes.summary: An intrinsic electrical system and a pressure cycle coordinate each heartbeat.

nodes.label: SA node pacemaker
nodes.summary: The sinoatrial node is the heart's intrinsic pacemaker, spontaneously depolarizing to set heart rate.

nodes.label: AV node and delay
nodes.summary: The AV node delays the impulse, allowing atria to finish contracting before ventricles.

nodes.label: Bundle of His and Purkinje fibers
nodes.summary: The bundle of His and Purkinje fibers rapidly spread excitation through the ventricles for coordinated contraction.

nodes.label: Pacemaker autorhythmicity
nodes.summary: Pacemaker cells have an unstable resting potential (funny current) that drives spontaneous depolarization.

nodes.label: Cardiac myocyte action potential
nodes.summary: Ventricular myocytes show a long plateau phase from Ca2+ influx that prevents tetanus and sustains contraction.

nodes.label: ECG waves
nodes.summary: The P wave is atrial depolarization, the QRS complex ventricular depolarization, and the T wave ventricular repolarization.

nodes.label: Autonomic heart rate control
nodes.summary: Sympathetic input speeds and parasympathetic (vagal) input slows the heart rate.

nodes.label: Systole and diastole
nodes.summary: Systole is ventricular contraction (ejection); diastole is relaxation and filling.

nodes.label: Cardiac output (CO = HR x SV)
nodes.summary: Cardiac output equals heart rate times stroke volume, the volume of blood pumped per minute.

nodes.label: Frank-Starling mechanism
nodes.summary: Greater venous return stretches the ventricle, increasing contractile force and stroke volume.

nodes.label: Blood vessels
nodes.summary: Arteries, capillaries, and veins have structures matched to their roles in distribution, exchange, and return.

nodes.label: Arteries and arterioles
nodes.summary: Arteries carry blood away from the heart under high pressure with thick elastic, muscular walls.

nodes.label: Capillaries and exchange
nodes.summary: Single-cell-thick capillaries are the sites of gas, nutrient, and waste exchange with tissues.

nodes.label: Veins and venous return
nodes.summary: Thin-walled veins with valves return blood at low pressure, aided by skeletal muscle and respiratory pumps.

nodes.label: Flow velocity and cross-sectional area
nodes.summary: Blood flows slowest in capillaries because their huge combined cross-sectional area minimizes velocity, aiding exchange.

nodes.label: Blood pressure (systolic/diastolic)
nodes.summary: Blood pressure is highest in arteries during systole and lowest during diastole, falling along the vascular tree.

nodes.label: Peripheral resistance and vasoconstriction
nodes.summary: Arteriolar diameter changes alter resistance and thus blood pressure and flow distribution.

nodes.label: Starling forces (filtration/reabsorption)
nodes.summary: Hydrostatic pressure drives fluid out at the arterial end and osmotic pressure pulls it back at the venous end.

nodes.label: Baroreceptor reflex
nodes.summary: Baroreceptors sense arterial pressure and trigger autonomic adjustments to maintain it.

nodes.label: Blood composition
nodes.summary: Blood is a connective tissue of plasma and formed elements carrying out transport and defense.

nodes.label: Plasma
nodes.summary: Plasma is the fluid matrix carrying proteins (albumin, antibodies, clotting factors), ions, nutrients, and wastes.

nodes.label: Plasma oncotic pressure (albumin)
nodes.summary: Plasma proteins, chiefly albumin, generate colloid osmotic pressure that pulls fluid back into capillaries.

nodes.label: Erythrocytes
nodes.summary: Red blood cells are biconcave, anucleate cells packed with hemoglobin for oxygen transport.

nodes.label: Hemoglobin and oxygen carriage
nodes.summary: Hemoglobin reversibly binds up to four O2 with cooperative, sigmoidal binding.

nodes.label: Oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve
nodes.summary: The sigmoidal curve shifts right (lower affinity) with high CO2, H+, temperature, and 2,3-BPG (Bohr effect).

nodes.label: Fetal hemoglobin and curve shifts
nodes.summary: Fetal hemoglobin's higher O2 affinity (left-shifted curve) extracts oxygen from maternal blood at the placenta.

nodes.label: CO2 transport / bicarbonate buffer
nodes.summary: CO2 is carried mainly as bicarbonate formed via carbonic anhydrase, also buffering blood pH.

nodes.label: Leukocytes
nodes.summary: White blood cells defend against infection and are divided into granulocytes and agranulocytes.

nodes.label: Platelets and coagulation cascade
nodes.summary: Platelets form a plug and trigger a clotting cascade ending in fibrin, mediated by thrombin and clotting factors.

nodes.label: Clot retraction and fibrinolysis
nodes.summary: After healing, plasmin digests fibrin to dissolve the clot, restoring normal flow.

nodes.label: ABO and Rh blood groups
nodes.summary: ABO and Rh antigens on red cells determine compatibility and transfusion reactions.

nodes.label: Hematopoiesis
nodes.summary: Blood cells arise from bone marrow stem cells, with erythropoiesis stimulated by erythropoietin.

nodes.label: Respiratory system
nodes.summary: The respiratory system moves air to the alveoli for gas exchange and helps regulate blood pH.

nodes.label: Respiratory anatomy
nodes.summary: Conducting airways deliver air to the alveolar gas-exchange surface.

nodes.label: Airway pathway
nodes.summary: Air flows nose/mouth → pharynx → larynx → trachea → bronchi → bronchioles → alveoli.

nodes.label: Alveoli and surfactant
nodes.summary: Thin-walled alveoli maximize gas-exchange surface area; type II cells secrete surfactant to reduce surface tension.

nodes.label: Conducting vs respiratory zone
nodes.summary: The conducting zone warms, humidifies, and filters air without exchange; the respiratory zone performs gas exchange.

nodes.label: Mucociliary escalator
nodes.summary: Mucus traps particles and cilia sweep them out, protecting the lungs (immune/filtration role).

nodes.label: Ventilation mechanics
nodes.summary: Pressure changes driven by muscles move air in and out of the lungs.

nodes.label: Diaphragm and inspiration
nodes.summary: Diaphragm and external intercostal contraction expands the thorax, lowering pressure to draw air in.

nodes.label: Expiration
nodes.summary: Quiet expiration is passive elastic recoil; forced expiration uses abdominal and internal intercostal muscles.

nodes.label: Boyle's law in breathing
nodes.summary: Increasing thoracic volume decreases pressure (Boyle's law), driving airflow into the lungs.

nodes.label: Intrapleural pressure
nodes.summary: Negative intrapleural pressure keeps the lungs adhered to the chest wall and inflated.

nodes.label: Lung compliance and elastic recoil
nodes.summary: Compliance is how easily lungs stretch; surfactant and elastic fibers govern recoil and resistance to collapse.

nodes.label: Lung volumes and capacities
nodes.summary: Tidal volume, vital capacity, residual volume, and others quantify lung function by spirometry.

nodes.label: Gas exchange and transport
nodes.summary: Gases diffuse across the alveolar membrane down partial-pressure gradients.

nodes.label: Alveolar gas diffusion
nodes.summary: O2 and CO2 diffuse passively across the thin alveolar-capillary membrane down partial-pressure gradients.

nodes.label: Partial pressure gradients
nodes.summary: High alveolar PO2 and low blood PO2 drive O2 uptake; the reverse gradient drives CO2 release.

nodes.label: Ventilation-perfusion matching
nodes.summary: Local airflow (ventilation) and blood flow (perfusion) are matched so gas exchange stays efficient across the lung.

nodes.label: Respiratory regulation
nodes.summary: Breathing rate is controlled by brainstem centers responding to blood chemistry.

nodes.label: Medullary respiratory center
nodes.summary: The medulla oblongata sets the basic involuntary breathing rhythm.

nodes.label: Chemoreceptors (CO2/pH drive)
nodes.summary: Central and peripheral chemoreceptors increase ventilation mainly in response to rising CO2 and falling pH.

nodes.label: Respiratory pH regulation
nodes.summary: Adjusting ventilation changes CO2 and thus blood pH through the bicarbonate buffer system.

nodes.label: Respiratory immune and filtration roles
nodes.summary: Mucus, cilia, alveolar macrophages, and trapped particulates protect the lung as a defensive interface.

nodes.label: Respiratory roles in homeostasis
nodes.summary: The respiratory tract also dissipates heat and water, contributing to thermoregulation.

nodes.label: Immune system
nodes.summary: The immune system defends the body through nonspecific innate and specific adaptive responses.

nodes.label: Innate immunity
nodes.summary: Innate immunity provides rapid, nonspecific defense through barriers and cellular responses.

nodes.label: Physical and chemical barriers
nodes.summary: Skin, mucous membranes, stomach acid, and lysozyme block and kill pathogens before infection.

nodes.label: Phagocytes (macrophages, neutrophils)
nodes.summary: Macrophages and neutrophils engulf and destroy pathogens nonspecifically.

nodes.label: Inflammatory response
nodes.summary: Histamine-driven vasodilation and increased permeability recruit immune cells, causing redness, heat, swelling, and pain.

nodes.label: Complement system
nodes.summary: The complement cascade opsonizes pathogens, recruits cells, and lyses membranes.

nodes.label: Natural killer cells
nodes.summary: NK cells kill virus-infected and tumor cells lacking normal MHC I.

nodes.label: Interferons
nodes.summary: Interferons are antiviral signaling proteins that limit viral spread to neighboring cells.

nodes.label: Fever and systemic response
nodes.summary: Pyrogens reset the hypothalamic set point to raise body temperature, slowing pathogen growth.

nodes.label: Adaptive immunity
nodes.summary: Adaptive immunity provides specific, memory-forming defense via lymphocytes.

nodes.label: Antigens and epitopes
nodes.summary: An antigen is a molecule recognized by the immune system; its epitope is the specific region a receptor or antibody binds.

nodes.label: B cells and humoral immunity
nodes.summary: B cells differentiate into plasma cells that secrete antibodies against extracellular pathogens.

nodes.label: Antibody structure and function
nodes.summary: Antibodies are Y-shaped proteins with variable antigen-binding regions that neutralize, opsonize, and agglutinate.

nodes.label: Antibody diversity (VDJ recombination)
nodes.summary: Recombination of variable gene segments generates a vast repertoire of antigen-binding specificities.

nodes.label: T cells and cell-mediated immunity
nodes.summary: Helper T cells (CD4) coordinate the response and cytotoxic T cells (CD8) kill infected cells.

nodes.label: MHC I and II antigen presentation
nodes.summary: MHC I displays intracellular antigens to CD8 cells; MHC II on APCs displays extracellular antigens to CD4 cells.

nodes.label: Antigen-presenting cells and activation
nodes.summary: Dendritic cells, macrophages, and B cells present antigen on MHC II to activate helper T cells, bridging innate and adaptive immunity.

nodes.label: Clonal selection and memory
nodes.summary: Antigen activates matching lymphocytes that proliferate into effector and long-lived memory cells.

nodes.label: Primary vs secondary response
nodes.summary: The secondary response is faster and stronger than the primary because of pre-existing memory cells.

nodes.label: Active vs passive immunity
nodes.summary: Active immunity is self-made (infection/vaccine); passive immunity is received antibodies (placenta, antiserum).

nodes.label: Self-tolerance and autoimmunity
nodes.summary: Lymphocytes reactive to self are normally eliminated; failure causes autoimmunity.

nodes.label: Lymphatic system
nodes.summary: The lymphatic system drains interstitial fluid and houses immune tissues.

nodes.label: Lymph drainage and circulation
nodes.summary: Lymphatic vessels return interstitial fluid to the bloodstream via the thoracic duct.

nodes.label: Lymphoid organs
nodes.summary: Lymph nodes, spleen, thymus, and bone marrow generate, mature, and deploy immune cells.

nodes.label: Lacteals and lipid transport
nodes.summary: Intestinal lacteals absorb dietary fats as chylomicrons and carry them into the lymphatic circulation.

nodes.label: Digestive system
nodes.summary: The digestive system mechanically and chemically breaks down food and absorbs nutrients.

nodes.label: Mouth, esophagus, stomach
nodes.summary: The upper GI tract ingests, fragments, and begins chemical digestion of food.

nodes.label: Mouth and salivary amylase
nodes.summary: Mastication and salivary amylase begin mechanical and starch digestion in the mouth.

nodes.label: Swallowing and peristalsis
nodes.summary: The esophagus moves the bolus to the stomach by peristaltic smooth-muscle waves.

nodes.label: Stomach acid and pepsin
nodes.summary: Parietal cells secrete HCl and chief cells secrete pepsinogen, which becomes pepsin to begin protein digestion.

nodes.label: Gastric cell types
nodes.summary: Parietal (HCl, intrinsic factor), chief (pepsinogen), mucous (mucus), and G cells (gastrin) line the stomach.

nodes.label: GI tract wall layers
nodes.summary: The gut wall has mucosa, submucosa, muscularis externa, and serosa from lumen outward.

nodes.label: Small and large intestine
nodes.summary: The intestines complete digestion and absorb nutrients and water.

nodes.label: Small intestine absorption
nodes.summary: The duodenum, jejunum, and ileum complete digestion and absorb most nutrients across villi and microvilli.

nodes.label: Villi and microvilli
nodes.summary: Villi and microvilli (brush border) hugely increase absorptive surface area.

nodes.label: Nutrient absorption pathways
nodes.summary: Monosaccharides and amino acids enter blood capillaries; fats enter lacteals as chylomicrons.

nodes.label: Intestinal motility (segmentation)
nodes.summary: Segmentation mixes chyme with enzymes while peristalsis advances it along the intestine.

nodes.label: Large intestine and water reabsorption
nodes.summary: The large intestine reabsorbs water and electrolytes and houses the microbiome that produces vitamin K.

nodes.label: Rectum, anus, and elimination
nodes.summary: Feces are stored in the rectum and expelled through the anal sphincters via the defecation reflex.

nodes.label: Accessory organs and enzymes
nodes.summary: The liver, pancreas, and gallbladder secrete enzymes and bile to aid digestion.

nodes.label: Pancreatic enzymes and bicarbonate
nodes.summary: The exocrine pancreas secretes amylase, lipase, proteases, and bicarbonate to neutralize chyme.

nodes.label: Bile and fat emulsification
nodes.summary: Liver-made, gallbladder-stored bile emulsifies fats to increase the surface area for lipase.

nodes.label: Liver functions
nodes.summary: The liver detoxifies, makes plasma proteins, stores glycogen, and processes absorbed nutrients via the hepatic portal system.

nodes.label: Hepatic portal system
nodes.summary: Nutrient-rich blood from the intestines drains to the liver via the hepatic portal vein before reaching general circulation.

nodes.label: Digestive enzyme summary
nodes.summary: Carbohydrases, proteases, lipases, and nucleases each hydrolyze a specific class of macromolecule.

nodes.label: Digestive regulation
nodes.summary: Hormones and nerves coordinate the timing of digestive secretion and motility.

nodes.label: GI hormones (gastrin, secretin, CCK)
nodes.summary: Gastrin stimulates acid, secretin triggers bicarbonate, and CCK triggers bile and enzyme release.

nodes.label: Neural control of digestion
nodes.summary: The parasympathetic system and enteric nervous system promote digestive motility and secretion.

nodes.label: Hunger and satiety regulation
nodes.summary: Hypothalamic centers integrate leptin, ghrelin, and insulin signals to regulate appetite and energy balance.

nodes.label: Excretory (renal) system
nodes.summary: The kidneys filter blood to excrete wastes and regulate fluid, electrolyte, and acid-base balance.

nodes.label: Kidney and nephron anatomy
nodes.summary: The nephron is the functional filtering unit organized within cortex and medulla.

nodes.label: Kidney structure (cortex/medulla)
nodes.summary: The kidney's cortex and medulla house nephrons whose collecting ducts drain to the renal pelvis and ureter.

nodes.label: Nephron components
nodes.summary: A nephron comprises Bowman's capsule, proximal tubule, loop of Henle, distal tubule, and collecting duct.

nodes.label: Glomerulus and Bowman's capsule
nodes.summary: The glomerular capillary tuft filters plasma into Bowman's capsule by hydrostatic pressure.

nodes.label: Afferent/efferent arterioles
nodes.summary: Afferent and efferent arterioles regulate glomerular pressure and filtration rate.

nodes.label: Juxtaglomerular apparatus
nodes.summary: The juxtaglomerular apparatus senses blood pressure and Na+ delivery and secretes renin to regulate filtration.

nodes.label: Ureters, bladder, and micturition
nodes.summary: Urine drains via ureters to the bladder and is expelled through the urethra in the micturition reflex.

nodes.label: Urine formation processes
nodes.summary: Urine forms through filtration, reabsorption, and secretion along the nephron.

nodes.label: Glomerular filtration
nodes.summary: High glomerular pressure filters water and small solutes while retaining blood cells and large proteins.

nodes.label: Glomerular filtration rate and clearance
nodes.summary: GFR is the filtrate volume formed per minute; clearance measures how completely a substance is removed from plasma.

nodes.label: Tubular reabsorption
nodes.summary: The proximal tubule reabsorbs most glucose, amino acids, ions, and water back into the blood.

nodes.label: Tubular secretion
nodes.summary: The tubules actively secrete H+, K+, and drugs from the blood into the filtrate.

nodes.label: Loop of Henle countercurrent multiplier
nodes.summary: The countercurrent multiplier establishes a medullary osmotic gradient enabling concentrated urine.

nodes.label: Vasa recta countercurrent exchange
nodes.summary: The vasa recta run alongside the loop and preserve the medullary gradient by countercurrent exchange.

nodes.label: Urine concentration and collecting duct
nodes.summary: Water is reabsorbed from the collecting duct down the medullary gradient under ADH control.

nodes.label: Renal homeostatic regulation
nodes.summary: Hormones tune kidney handling of water and electrolytes to maintain homeostasis.

nodes.label: ADH and water balance
nodes.summary: ADH increases collecting-duct water permeability (via aquaporins), concentrating urine when dehydrated.

nodes.label: Aldosterone and sodium balance
nodes.summary: Aldosterone increases Na+ (and water) reabsorption and K+ secretion in the distal nephron.

nodes.label: Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system
nodes.summary: Low blood pressure triggers renin → angiotensin II (vasoconstriction) → aldosterone to raise pressure and volume.

nodes.label: ANP and volume regulation
nodes.summary: Atrial natriuretic peptide promotes Na+ and water excretion to lower blood volume and pressure, opposing RAAS.

nodes.label: Renal acid-base regulation
nodes.summary: The kidney regulates pH by reabsorbing bicarbonate and secreting hydrogen ions.

nodes.label: Nitrogenous waste (urea)
nodes.summary: Ammonia from protein catabolism is converted to urea in the liver and excreted by the kidney.

nodes.label: Musculoskeletal system
nodes.summary: The muscular and skeletal systems together support the body, protect organs, and generate movement.

nodes.label: Muscle tissue types
nodes.summary: Skeletal, cardiac, and smooth muscle differ in structure and control.

nodes.label: Skeletal muscle
nodes.summary: Skeletal muscle is striated, multinucleate, and under voluntary somatic control.

nodes.label: Cardiac muscle
nodes.summary: Cardiac muscle is striated, branched, and involuntary with intercalated discs.

nodes.label: Smooth muscle
nodes.summary: Smooth muscle is nonstriated and involuntary, lining hollow organs and vessels.

nodes.label: Smooth muscle contraction (calmodulin)
nodes.summary: Smooth muscle uses Ca2+-calmodulin to activate myosin light-chain kinase rather than troponin, allowing slow sustained tone.

nodes.label: Muscle contraction
nodes.summary: Skeletal muscle contracts via the sliding-filament mechanism triggered by neural input.

nodes.label: Muscle fiber organization
nodes.summary: A muscle is bundles of fibers (cells) containing myofibrils made of repeating sarcomeres.

nodes.label: Sarcomere structure
nodes.summary: The sarcomere (Z-line to Z-line) is the contractile unit with overlapping actin (thin) and myosin (thick) filaments.

nodes.label: Sarcomere bands (A, I, H, Z, M)
nodes.summary: The A band stays constant while the I band and H zone shorten during contraction, marking filament overlap.

nodes.label: Sliding filament theory
nodes.summary: Myosin heads pull actin inward, shortening the sarcomere while filament lengths stay constant.

nodes.label: Cross-bridge cycle
nodes.summary: ATP-powered myosin binds, pulls (power stroke), releases, and re-cocks against actin in a cycle.

nodes.label: Rigor mortis and ATP role
nodes.summary: Without ATP, myosin cannot detach from actin, causing the stiff cross-bridges of rigor mortis.

nodes.label: Excitation-contraction coupling
nodes.summary: An action potential triggers Ca2+ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum, exposing actin binding sites.

nodes.label: T-tubules and sarcoplasmic reticulum
nodes.summary: T-tubules conduct the action potential into the fiber to trigger Ca2+ release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.

nodes.label: Troponin and tropomyosin regulation
nodes.summary: Ca2+ binds troponin, moving tropomyosin off actin to permit cross-bridge formation.

nodes.label: Neuromuscular junction
nodes.summary: Acetylcholine released at the NMJ depolarizes the muscle fiber to initiate contraction.

nodes.label: Motor units and summation
nodes.summary: A motor neuron and its fibers form a motor unit; recruitment and frequency summation grade force.

nodes.label: Twitch, summation, and tetanus
nodes.summary: Rapid repeated stimulation fuses individual twitches into a sustained maximal tetanic contraction.

nodes.label: Length-tension relationship
nodes.summary: Maximal force occurs at the sarcomere length giving optimal actin-myosin overlap.

nodes.label: Muscle energy systems
nodes.summary: Muscles use creatine phosphate, anaerobic glycolysis, and oxidative phosphorylation depending on demand.

nodes.label: Oxygen debt and fatigue
nodes.summary: Anaerobic exertion accumulates lactate and an oxygen debt repaid afterward; depletion and acidosis cause fatigue.

nodes.label: Slow vs fast twitch fibers
nodes.summary: Slow oxidative fibers resist fatigue while fast glycolytic fibers contract powerfully but tire quickly.

nodes.label: Skeletal system
nodes.summary: Bones provide support, protection, movement leverage, mineral storage, and blood-cell formation.

nodes.label: Bone structure (compact/spongy)
nodes.summary: Compact bone is dense with osteons; spongy bone contains marrow within trabeculae.

nodes.label: Bone matrix (collagen and hydroxyapatite)
nodes.summary: Bone combines flexible collagen with rigid calcium hydroxyapatite mineral for strength and elasticity.

nodes.label: Bone cells (osteoblast/clast/cyte)
nodes.summary: Osteoblasts build bone, osteoclasts resorb it, and osteocytes maintain it.

nodes.label: Bone remodeling and calcium store
nodes.summary: Continuous remodeling lets bone serve as a calcium reservoir controlled by PTH and calcitonin.

nodes.label: Bone formation and growth
nodes.summary: Most bones form by endochondral ossification, replacing a cartilage model and lengthening at growth plates.

nodes.label: Cartilage, ligaments, and joints
nodes.summary: Cartilage cushions joints; ligaments connect bones and tendons connect muscle to bone for movement.

nodes.label: Joint types and lever systems
nodes.summary: Synovial, cartilaginous, and fibrous joints permit varying movement; antagonistic muscle pairs act on bony levers.

nodes.label: Axial vs appendicular skeleton
nodes.summary: The axial skeleton (skull, spine, ribs) protects organs; the appendicular skeleton enables limb movement.

nodes.label: Reproductive system
nodes.summary: The reproductive system produces gametes and sex hormones and supports development.

nodes.label: Male reproductive system
nodes.summary: The male system produces sperm and testosterone and delivers gametes.

nodes.label: Male anatomy and sperm path
nodes.summary: Sperm travel testes → epididymis → vas deferens → ejaculatory duct → urethra, mixing with accessory gland secretions.

nodes.label: Male accessory glands and semen
nodes.summary: The seminal vesicles, prostate, and bulbourethral glands add fluid and nutrients to form semen.

nodes.label: Spermatogenesis
nodes.summary: Spermatogenesis in seminiferous tubules continuously produces four haploid sperm per meiosis.

nodes.label: Sperm structure
nodes.summary: A sperm has an acrosome-capped head with DNA, a midpiece packed with mitochondria, and a propelling flagellum.

nodes.label: Testosterone regulation
nodes.summary: LH drives Leydig-cell testosterone and FSH supports Sertoli cells for sperm production.

nodes.label: Female reproductive system
nodes.summary: The female system produces eggs and hormones and supports pregnancy.

nodes.label: Female anatomy
nodes.summary: Ovaries release eggs to the fallopian tubes leading to the uterus where implantation occurs.

nodes.label: Oogenesis
nodes.summary: Oogenesis produces one egg and polar bodies, arresting in meiosis until ovulation and fertilization.

nodes.label: Follicle and corpus luteum
nodes.summary: The ovarian follicle matures and releases the egg, then becomes the corpus luteum secreting progesterone.

nodes.label: Menstrual / ovarian cycle
nodes.summary: FSH/LH and estrogen/progesterone cycle through follicular, ovulatory, and luteal phases.

nodes.label: Uterine (endometrial) cycle
nodes.summary: The endometrium proliferates under estrogen, becomes secretory under progesterone, then sheds as menstruation if no implantation.

nodes.label: LH surge and ovulation
nodes.summary: Rising estrogen positively feeds back to trigger an LH surge that causes ovulation.

nodes.label: Pregnancy hormones (hCG)
nodes.summary: hCG from the embryo maintains the corpus luteum and progesterone in early pregnancy.

nodes.label: Fertilization and early development
nodes.summary: Fertilization initiates development into a multicellular organism.

nodes.label: Fertilization
nodes.summary: A sperm fertilizes the egg, restoring diploidy and triggering the cortical reaction to block polyspermy.

nodes.label: Placenta and gestation
nodes.summary: The placenta exchanges gases, nutrients, and wastes between maternal and fetal blood and secretes pregnancy hormones.

nodes.label: Sexual differentiation
nodes.summary: The SRY gene and sex hormones direct development of male or female reproductive structures.

nodes.label: Skin (integumentary system)
nodes.summary: The skin protects the body, regulates temperature, and supports sensation and homeostasis.

nodes.label: Skin structure
nodes.summary: The skin has layered epidermis, dermis, and underlying hypodermis with appendages.

nodes.label: Epidermis, dermis, hypodermis
nodes.summary: The epidermis is a keratinized barrier, the dermis holds vessels and nerves, and the hypodermis stores fat.

nodes.label: Skin appendages
nodes.summary: Hair, nails, and sweat/sebaceous glands assist protection, sensation, and thermoregulation.

nodes.label: Skin functions
nodes.summary: Skin contributes to protection, thermoregulation, sensation, and homeostasis.

nodes.label: Thermoregulation
nodes.summary: Sweating and vasodilation/vasoconstriction of dermal vessels and piloerection regulate body temperature.

nodes.label: Barrier and immune defense
nodes.summary: Skin blocks pathogens and water loss, serving as a first-line innate immune barrier.

nodes.label: Cutaneous sensation
nodes.summary: Dermal receptors detect touch, pressure, temperature, and pain, providing sensory input to the CNS.

nodes.label: Vitamin D synthesis
nodes.summary: UV light converts a skin precursor to vitamin D, supporting calcium absorption and homeostasis.

nodes.label: System integration and homeostasis
nodes.summary: Organ systems work together through feedback loops to maintain a stable internal environment.

nodes.label: Homeostasis principles
nodes.summary: Homeostasis is maintained by feedback control across coordinating systems.

nodes.label: Negative and positive feedback in physiology
nodes.summary: Negative feedback restores set points while positive feedback amplifies until an endpoint.

nodes.label: Set point and effectors
nodes.summary: A receptor detects deviation from a set point, a control center compares it, and effectors correct it.

nodes.label: Cross-system coordination
nodes.summary: The nervous and endocrine systems integrate the actions of all organ systems.

nodes.label: Neuroendocrine integration
nodes.summary: The hypothalamus links rapid neural signaling to sustained endocrine control of homeostasis.

nodes.label: Fluid and electrolyte integration
nodes.summary: Cardiovascular, renal, and endocrine systems jointly regulate blood volume, pressure, and electrolytes.

nodes.label: Acid-base integration
nodes.summary: Respiratory and renal systems together regulate blood pH via CO2 and bicarbonate.

nodes.label: Integrated thermoregulation
nodes.summary: The hypothalamus directs skin, muscle, and vascular responses to defend core temperature.

nodes.label: Integrated energy and glucose balance
nodes.summary: Digestive, hepatic, pancreatic, and adrenal signals jointly keep blood glucose and energy supply stable.

nodes.label: Circadian regulation
nodes.summary: The hypothalamic clock and melatonin entrain daily rhythms in hormones, temperature, and sleep.
